questions.question: Define the following tree terminology: (i) Root node (ii) Leaf node (iii) Degree of a node (iv) Level of a node
keyPoints: Root node: The topmost node with no parent - starting point of tree
Leaf node: Node with degree zero (no children) - terminal node
Degree of a node: Number of children a node has
Level of a node: Distance from root node (root at level 0)

questions.question: Explain the difference between a tree and a linear data structure. Give two examples of each.
keyPoints: Tree is non-linear, hierarchical; linear stores sequentially
Tree allows one-to-many relationships; linear is one-to-one
Examples of linear: Array, Linked List, Stack, Queue
Examples of tree: Binary Tree, Binary Search Tree, Family tree, Org chart

questions.question: What is a binary tree? State its fundamental property regarding node degrees.
keyPoints: Binary tree: Tree data structure where each node has at most two children
Children are typically called left child and right child
Maximum degree of any node is 2
This distinguishes it from general trees which have no degree restriction

questions.question: Define height and depth of a tree. How are they related?
keyPoints: Height of tree: Number of edges on longest path from root to leaf
Depth of node: Number of edges from root to that node
Height of tree = Maximum depth of any node
Root has depth 0; leaf nodes determine height

questions.question: Explain the concept of parent-child relationship in trees with an example.
keyPoints: Parent node: Has one or more child nodes below it
Child node: Directly connected to parent, has degree based on its children
Each child has exactly one parent (except root)
Example: In a family tree, a parent has children, those children are siblings to each other

questions.question: What is a subtree? How is it formed?
keyPoints: Subtree: A portion of a tree that forms a complete tree structure
Formed by selecting any node as the root of the subtree
Includes that node and ALL its descendants
Every node (except root) is root of some subtree

questions.question: Differentiate between internal nodes and leaf nodes. Can a tree have only leaf nodes?
keyPoints: Internal node: Has at least one child (degree > 0)
Leaf node: Has no children (degree = 0)
A tree cannot have only leaf nodes - must have at least one internal node (the root)
Root is internal unless tree has only one node

questions.question: Discuss the basic concepts of trees as a non-linear data structure. Explain why trees are preferred over linear structures for representing hierarchical relationships. Provide real-world examples.
keyPoints: Trees are hierarchical/non-linear with parent-child relationships
Linear structures (array, linked list) store sequentially
Trees allow branching - one parent can have multiple children
Efficient for representing: organizational structures, file systems, family trees, XML/HTML DOM
Operations like search, insert faster in balanced trees O(log n) vs O(n) in linear
Tree provides logical structure reflecting real-world hierarchies
Examples: Computer file system (root, folders, files), HTML document structure, Company org chart, Decision trees in AI

questions.question: Explain all key tree terminology including root, parent, child, sibling, ancestor, descendant, leaf, degree, level, height, depth, path, and subtree with appropriate examples.
keyPoints: Root: Topmost node, no parent
Parent: Node with children
Child: Node with parent
Sibling: Nodes with same parent
Ancestor: Any node on path from root to node
Descendant: Nodes reachable from given node
Leaf: Node with no children (degree 0)
Degree: Number of children
Level: Distance from root (root=0)
Height: Longest path from root to leaf
Depth: Distance from root to node
Path: Sequence of connected nodes
Subtree: Node and all its descendants

questions.question: Prove that a tree with n nodes has exactly (n-1) edges. Also explain why trees are called connected acyclic graphs.
keyPoints: A tree with n nodes has n-1 edges - proof by induction
Base case: n=1 has 0 edges (1-1=0)
Inductive step: Adding one node to existing tree adds exactly one edge
Trees are connected: All nodes reachable from root
Trees are acyclic: No cycles/loops exist
If connected graph has n nodes and n-1 edges, it must be a tree
These two properties define trees uniquely

questions.question: Compare binary trees with general trees. Discuss the advantages of the binary tree constraint and its applications.
keyPoints: Binary tree: Max 2 children per node (left and right)
General tree: No restriction on number of children
Binary constraint simplifies implementation and algorithms
Easier to store (array or two pointers)
Used in: Binary Search Trees (efficient searching), Heaps (priority queues), Expression trees, Huffman coding trees
Binary trees can represent any general tree through conversion
The restriction enables efficient O(log n) operations in balanced variants
